info: The number of EVENTS available for export. NOTE: This is not the number of RESULTS available for export but rather the number of EVENTS. Therefore, when running in smart mode or fast mode and performing a transforming search such as stats or timechart then the available_count will always be 0, because there are no events available. When running in verbose mode, and performing a stats or timechart, then the available_count will show you how many EVENTS you can export from the completed search.

If you enable Dynamic Data Self-Storage (DDSS) to export your aged ingested data, the oldest data is moved to your Amazon S3 or Google Cloud Storage account in the same region as your Splunk Cloud Platform deployment before it is deleted from the index.  Note the following:  You are responsible for payments for your use of Amazon S3 or Google Cloud Storage. Aged data is exported unencrypted to your Amazon S3 or Google Cloud Storage account
description: Dynamic Data: Self-Storage

info: How many slices were decompressed to fetch the raw events from (i.e., after searching in tsidx, we know exactly which slices to decompress to get the raw events). The larger the difference between this field and total_slices, the sparser the search. Slices: Collection of compressed (gzipped) blocks, called slices, Concatenated together in a rawdata/journal.gz Think “cat chunkA.gzchunkB.gz...chunkN.gz> journal.gz”). Slices contain the actual raw events.  Pool of concatenated slices allows be seeked into Locations offsets are pointed to by the values array pointers in tsidx.  Such organization allows us to zoom in to the right slice reduces the amount of decompression time & volume compared to having a single, massive rawdatafile.

info: Indicates if the results of the search were returned fully or partially. Often tied with has_error_warn when it indicates that errors or warnings were encountered. If the search is not fully completed then it has returned partial results. info=completed and fully_completed_search=false, indicating that the search was not truly successful

This tells us if there were errors or warnings that were encountered when the search was executed. These errors or warnings can be found in var/log/splunk/search_messages.log.  An example of an ERROR encountered for a running search would be where the search cannot distribute to all indexers.

info:
The status of the search:
1. info=completed means that the search completed successfully
2. info=cancelled/cancel means that the search was cancelled by the user
3. info=failed means that the search failed and did not complete successfully for some reason
4. info=pause means that the user who ran the search has paused the search while it is running.
5. info=resume means that the user who had paused a search, has resumed the search
6. info=finalize means that the search which was running was finalized either by the user, or automatically based on Splunk's auto-finalize rules for the user's role, or the WLM rules applied.
7. info=bad_request means that the search that was run had an error in the SPL, and it cannot be executed.
There are other values for info, but these are the main ones.

Shows the SPL search string executed

This ACTUAL earliest time of the searched time window, after the search has been parsed for execution. (reflected in epoch time) This value can be different to api_et if the users time range picker is set to one value, but the user has explicitly used the earliest and latest time modifiers in the actual search string.  The same thing happens for a saved search where the dispatch.earliest and dispatch.latest values in savedsearches.conf are set to specific values, but the string SPL uses the earliest and latesttime modifiers in the search string. For example, if a users time range picker is set to Last Week but the user enters earliest=-15m latest=now into their search string, then api_et will reflect the epoch time for the beginning of Last Week (from the time range picker) and the search_et will reflect the epoch time for 15 minutes ago, which comes from the earliest time modifier value in the search string, and which is what gets applied to the search when it is parsed for execution.
This is the ID assigned to the search from the search head or search head cluster. This can be used to trace the search on the indexers also.
The ACTUAL latest time of the searched time window, after the search has been parsed for execution. (reflected in epoch time) For detailed explanation, see search_et.

SmartStore maximizes data management flexibility while maintaining search performance by allowing compute (CPUs) and data storage to be independently scaled up or down based on business demands. It automatically evaluates users’ data access patterns via an application-aware cache and places actively accessed data in local storage for real-time analytics and unused/inactive data in lower-cost, remote storage.

info: Workload pricing aligns your investments in Splunk with how you use it to derive insights from your data. The primary pricing factor in this model is the compute capacity consumed for search and analytics workloads rather than the data volume ingested. Gain visibility into your license consumption and control how your total compute capacity gets used across various use cases and Splunk capabilities. This model is available for our cloud products and some of our on-premises offerings.
